---
title: "Drainage-aware topological evaluation of generative terrain"
subtitle: "Naming the axis of difference between generated and real drainage networks"
author:
  - name: "[Author 1]"
    email: "[email]"
    affiliations:
      - name: "[Institution]"
        department: "[Department]"
  - name: "[Author 2]"
    affiliations:
      - name: "[Institution]"
date: last-modified
abstract: |
  Generative models now synthesize digital elevation models from text, sketches, or coarse priors, but they are evaluated almost entirely with optical metrics such as the Fréchet Inception Distance, which measure how a heightmap looks and say little about how its surface would drain. We introduce a drainage-aware evaluation regime that measures this directly. The method reads the flow-accumulation field of a digital elevation model rather than its elevation, builds a merge tree on the donor graph of the D8 flow routing, and summarizes the channel network by the H0 persistence diagram at a drainage density calibrated to the National Hydrography Dataset. Populations of real and generated diagrams are compared with a sliced-Wasserstein-kernel maximum mean discrepancy test against a spatial by-tile null that respects the autocorrelation of terrain. A short theorem stack relates the construction to standard drainage analysis, and the construct is validated against independently mapped flowlines across three physiographic provinces with contrasting drainage character, two karst-bearing (the Cumberland Plateau and the Appalachian Highlands) and one not (the Coastal Plain). Using the MESA latent-diffusion generator as a worked case study, we find that every metric we evaluate, optical and topological alike, distinguishes generated from real terrain; the distinctive value of the flow-accumulation metric is interpretive, naming the axis of difference as topographic surface drainage organization where the optical metrics report only an unlocalized appearance gap. We characterize the metric's sensitivity through controlled perturbations and state its geographic scope and saddle-driven limitations explicitly.
bibliography: references.bib
# DRAFTING TARGET: html (no TeX needed on elis). The Copernicus/PDF target is a
# FINISHING STEP (ESurf accepts any-format PDF at initial submission): it needs
# tinytex (quarto install tinytex) and a vendored copernicus.cls, since no official
# quarto-journals Copernicus extension exists. Do NOT block drafting on it.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
# For the submission pass, swap to a PDF target wired to copernicus.cls:
#   format:
#     pdf:
#       documentclass: copernicus
#       keep-tex: true
---

# Introduction

Generative models of terrain have advanced quickly, moving from procedural and example-based authoring to conditional latent-diffusion and flow-matching systems that synthesize digital elevation models from text prompts, sketches, or coarse priors [@bornepons2025mesa]. The evaluation of these models has not kept pace with their architectures. The metrics in routine use are optical: the Fréchet Inception Distance and its kernel variant [@heusel2017fid; @binkowski2018kid], computed on rendered images, together with pixelwise and perceptual similarity scores and occasional user studies. These quantities measure how a heightmap looks, and they inherit feature spaces built for natural-image recognition rather than for landscapes [@kynkaanniemi2023].

What they do not measure is how the synthesized surface would drain. The organization of a drainage network, where channels begin, how they branch, and where they join, is a first-order property of real terrain and the property that any physical model built on a generated surface will inherit. It is also the property that decades of quantitative geomorphology have learned to summarize, through stream order and the branching ratios that follow from it [@horton1945; @strahler1957]. No standard generative-terrain metric reports anything on this axis, so a generator can produce surfaces that score well on appearance while routing water in ways that real landscapes do not, and current evaluation has no way to say so.

We propose an evaluation regime that measures drainage organization directly. The construction reads the flow-accumulation field of a DEM rather than its elevation, builds a merge tree on the donor graph of the flow routing, and summarizes it by the $H_0$ persistence diagram of the channel network at a calibrated, physically meaningful drainage density. Populations of these diagrams, one from real terrain and one from generated terrain, are compared with a kernel two-sample test against a spatial null that respects the autocorrelation of terrain. A small theorem stack connects the construction to standard drainage analysis: the merge tree is in bijection with the channel network, Horton-Strahler order is one of its coarsenings, and the persistence diagram is stable as a function of the accumulation field within an explicitly limited scope. We validate the construct against independently mapped National Hydrography Dataset flowlines, so that what the metric reports as drainage structure agrees with the drainage network as surveyed rather than as re-derived from our own routing.

We demonstrate the regime on MESA, a current latent-diffusion terrain generator [@bornepons2025mesa], as a worked case study across three physiographic provinces with contrasting drainage character (two of them karst-bearing and one, the Coastal Plain, not). The result is not that topology succeeds where appearance metrics fail. Every metric we evaluate, optical and topological alike, distinguishes MESA-generated terrain from real terrain. The distinction the topological metric provides is interpretive: where the optical metrics report an unlocalized fidelity gap entangled with overall appearance, the flow-accumulation metric names the axis of difference as topographic surface drainage organization and grounds that name in a validated correspondence with mapped channels. We also characterize honestly what the metric does and does not see, through controlled perturbations that bound its sensitivity, and we are explicit about the scope of the validity claim and about the role of saddle-driven routing instability in the measured divergence.

# Background and related work

Generative terrain models have advanced rapidly, moving from procedural and example-based authoring toward conditional latent-diffusion and flow-matching systems that synthesize digital elevation models from text, sketches, or low-resolution priors. Our worked case study uses MESA, a latent-diffusion terrain generator trained on global Copernicus elevation data [@bornepons2025mesa], which is representative of the current wave both in capability and in how it is assessed. The evaluation practice that accompanies these models has not kept pace with their architectures. Terrain papers report Fréchet Inception Distance and its kernel variant [@heusel2017fid; @binkowski2018kid] alongside pixelwise and perceptual similarity scores, slope and elevation histograms, and small user studies. These quantities are sensitive to the appearance of a heightmap, but they were designed for natural images and they say nothing, by construction, about how water would organize on the synthesized surface.

The limitation of optical metrics for terrain is specific and worth stating precisely, because it motivates the metric we propose without requiring us to dismiss the established measures. Fréchet Inception Distance summarizes the distance between two populations of Inception features, and those features were trained to separate ImageNet object categories rather than to encode the structure of a landscape. @kynkaanniemi2023 show that the metric tracks the presence of ImageNet classes in the feature representation rather than the domain-relevant content of the images, so a number that moves under a terrain edit does not by itself indicate which physical property changed. The most sophisticated terrain-specific alternative, the Perceived Terrain Realism Metric of @rajasekaran2022ptrm, combines geomorphon distributions with deep features and is calibrated to human perceptual judgments, which is a genuine advance over a borrowed image metric. It remains a measure of perceived realism rather than of drainage organization, and like the optical metrics it returns a single fidelity-style score that does not localize a discrepancy to a named geomorphic axis. The contribution of the present work is to add a measurement on exactly that axis, complementing rather than replacing the appearance-level scores: optical metrics can register that two terrain populations differ in fidelity, and the flow-accumulation metric can name the difference as one of topographic surface drainage organization.

A separate body of work has applied topological data analysis to the evaluation of generative models, and it supplies the methodological template we build on. The Geometry Score of @khrulkov2018 compares the persistent homology of point clouds sampled from real and generated manifolds; the Topology Distance of @horak2021 compares persistence diagrams of feature embeddings against Fréchet Inception Distance and its relatives; Manifold Topology Divergence frames the comparison of two data manifolds through persistence [@barannikov2021]; and more recent work evaluates generators through cubical homology and persistent entropy computed directly on the image grid [@suresh2025], which is the construction closest to a heightmap-grid pipeline. These methods establish that a population of persistence diagrams can serve as the carrier of an evaluation signal and that two-sample comparison of such populations is a sound way to assess a generator. None of them is concerned with terrain, and none chooses a filtration that reflects how a surface drains.

The complementary literature applies topological data analysis to terrain and to scalar fields, and it is mature. Persistence and its stability are foundational here: topological persistence and simplification [@edelsbrunner2002] and the stability of persistence diagrams under perturbation of the underlying function [@cohensteiner2007] are the results we lean on when we argue that our metric is well behaved as a function of the field it reads. On terrain specifically, persistence-based methods have been used to simplify elevation models and to extract geomorphic features, and recent geomorphology applications have used persistent homology to detect landslides from LiDAR-derived surfaces [@syzdykbayev2020]. The recurring choice in this line of work is to filter by elevation, which orders the surface by height. Elevation-sublevel persistence resolves ridges, peaks, and basin mergers well, but it conflates structures of equal height and does not directly express the hierarchy by which channels collect and combine.

Where these two literatures have been brought near each other is in the construction of merge trees and watershed hierarchies on terrain. TerraStream derives watershed hierarchies from elevation data using topological persistence on the height graph, with the hierarchy itself assembled by a downstream recursive basin decomposition [@danner2007terrastream]; batched union-find on the height graph computes persistence and contour trees efficiently at scale [@agarwal2006unionfind]; and the watershed-cut framework of @cousty2009watershed proves the equivalence between watersheds and minimum spanning forests on edge-weighted graphs. All of these constructions read a scalar that is either elevation or a generic image gradient, and all of them use spatial-grid adjacency on the underlying graph. The drainage-network reading of the resulting hierarchy, in the vocabulary of stream order, is not part of these frameworks. That vocabulary is itself long established: Horton's quantitative morphology of streams and drainage basins [@horton1945], Strahler's stream-ordering scheme [@strahler1957], and the scaling and self-organization of channel networks [@rodrigueziturbe1997] are the geomorphic theory our metric is designed to surface, and the extraction of drainage networks from elevation by thresholding flow accumulation [@ocallaghan1984] is the standard workflow our construction is meant to track.

This is the gap the present work bridges. To our knowledge no prior method poses a persistence-based distributional test as an evaluation regime for generative terrain, and no prior work uses flow accumulation as the filtration function for persistent homology on a digital elevation model. Our construction departs from the terrain merge-tree literature in the adjacency it uses, and this is the sharpest of the novelty points: the merge tree is built on the directed D8 flow graph by donor-based union-find, restricted to the channel mask and swept in ascending accumulation, rather than on the spatial-grid adjacency used by every prior terrain merge tree. The flow graph is the graph of a discrete vector field and the scalar is a global integral, drainage area, rather than a local elevation or contrast; neither reduces to the edge-weighted, spatially adjacent setting of the watershed-cut and hierarchical-watershed frameworks. We do not claim the underlying correspondence between this merge tree and the channel network as an unprecedented discovery. The surrounding scaffolding is dense, spanning the elevation-persistence pipeline of TerraStream, the watershed-as-minimum-spanning-forest result of @cousty2009watershed, and the tree-pruning combinatorics implicit in Horton-Strahler ordering, and our bijection lemma formalizes a folklore correspondence that prior work approached from these three sides without uniting them on the flow-accumulation scalar. The lemma is the clarifying step that makes the regime's outputs interpretable, and the contribution sits in the evaluation regime and the adjacency choice rather than in the lemma.

The statistical machinery for the test is taken off the shelf so that the contribution rests on the construction rather than on new estimators. We compare populations of persistence diagrams with a maximum mean discrepancy two-sample test [@gretton2012] using a sliced-Wasserstein kernel on diagrams [@carriere2017], and we assess significance against a null formed by splitting the data spatially by tile. This spatial by-tile null is a deliberate choice for the terrain setting: it guards against attributing to the generator a discrepancy that is really an artifact of geographic non-stationarity in the reference data. We validate the construct against National Hydrography Dataset flowlines [@usgsnhd] rather than against any product that shares algorithmic lineage with our own accumulation routing, so that the agreement we report reflects the channel network as independently mapped rather than a circular re-derivation. The result is an evaluation that names topographic surface drainage organization as the axis along which a generator's outputs depart from real terrain, sitting alongside the optical and perceptual metrics that detect an unlocalized appearance gap without identifying its source.

# The flow-accumulation persistence metric

## Construct: from DEM to persistence diagram

We build the metric from the flow-accumulation field of a digital elevation model, not from its elevation. A patch of DEM is first hydrologically conditioned by depression breaching and then routed with the single-flow-direction (D8) algorithm of @ocallaghan1984, which sends each cell to its steepest downslope neighbor and accumulates the number of cells draining through every cell. The resulting flow accumulation $A$ is a discrete proxy for contributing drainage area: it is large along channels and small on hillslopes and divides. We compute $A$ with WhiteboxTools [@lindsay2016whitebox]; the routing is implemented in `src/geo_tda/topo_eval/hydrology.py`.

Both depression breaching and D8 routing assume a continuous routing surface across which water flows downslope to a single neighbor. In karst, this assumption can be violated: sinkholes can capture surface flow and route it subsurface through dissolution conduits, so a closed depression that breaching treats as a routing obstacle may in reality drain underground. This bounds the external validity of the metric in karst settings, but it does not compromise the internal validity of the generated-to-real comparison, because the comparison is symmetric in its blindness to subsurface flow. The reference network we validate against, mapped National Hydrography Dataset flowlines, is itself a surface product that does not trace conduit flow, so both the reference and the DEM-derived network read only the surface drainage; the comparison therefore remains fair, and the limitation is one of geographic scope rather than of the test.

The topological object is a merge tree built on the donor graph of the flow field. The donor graph connects each cell to the cells whose flow enters it, its D8 donors, so adjacency follows the drainage routing rather than the spatial grid. We restrict attention to the channel mask $\{A \ge \tau\}$ and sweep its cells in ascending $A$: a new connected component is born at a channel head (a cell with no donors in the mask), and two or more components die into one at a confluence (a cell where distinct donor components meet). The merge tree records this birth-and-confluence history, and its $H_0$ persistence diagram records, for each component, the accumulation at which it is born and the accumulation at which it merges. The merge tree and diagram are computed in `src/geo_tda/topo_eval/merge_tree.py`.

Building adjacency on the flow graph rather than on grid neighbors is the central methodological choice, and it has a concrete benefit beyond fidelity to hydrology: the donor graph of a drainage network is a forest, so the channel mask carries no spurious one-dimensional cycles and its first Betti number is zero by construction. A merge tree built on grid (8-connected) adjacency instead admits many phantom cycles where channels run close together without being hydrologically connected; we quantify the magnitude of the artifacts the donor graph removes in the Results.

## Channelization threshold and scale invariance

The single free parameter of the construction is the channelization threshold $\tau$. Rather than fix it at an arbitrary cell count, we calibrate $\tau$ per patch so that the extracted channel network reproduces a common target drainage density, the median value of the National Hydrography Dataset over our study region, $1.732$ km km$^{-2}$; the calibration is `tau_for_target_density` in `pipeline.py`. Calibrating to a measured physical density rather than to a pixel count is what keeps the topological graph tied to real hydrology and makes the metric comparable across surfaces. Because D8 routing depends only on the ordering of elevations and not on their magnitudes, the accumulation field, and therefore the $H_0$ diagram, is invariant to any monotone rescaling of elevation: rescaling a patch by a factor of one thousand leaves the number of $H_0$ features unchanged (`substrate_comparability.json`). This scale invariance is essential when the comparison includes generated terrain, whose elevations live on an arbitrary normalized scale, and DEMs of differing resolution: matching $\tau$ to a drainage density extracts the same physical channel network in every case, so a difference between two diagrams reflects a difference in drainage organization rather than in units or grid spacing. @fig-pipeline traces the construction on a real patch, from the hillshade through the flow-accumulation field and the matched-density channel network to the resulting persistence diagram.

![From physical terrain to drainage topology on a real GLO-30 patch: (a) hillshade, (b) D8 flow accumulation, (c) channel network at the matched NHD density, (d) the H0 persistence diagram of the donor-graph merge tree. A physical ridgeline dictates the topological split.](figures/fig-pipeline.png){#fig-pipeline}

## Theoretical guarantees (statements here; proofs in Appendix A)

Four results connect the construction to standard drainage analysis. We state them here and prove them in Appendix A, and after each we give the physical reading that matters for a geomorphic audience.

A bijection lemma establishes that the donor-graph merge tree on the channel mask is in bijection with the channel network produced by the standard workflow of thresholding flow accumulation. The practical reading is that the topological object is the channel network itself rather than a proxy for it: births are channel heads and merges are confluences.

Theorem 1 shows that Horton-Strahler stream order is a coarsening of the merge tree, so that standard drainage statistics such as stream-order counts and the bifurcation ratio factor through the merge-tree combinatorial type. The reading is that the metric subsumes the bifurcation-ratio family that geomorphologists already use to summarize how a network branches.

Theorem 2 characterizes what the persistence diagram discards relative to the merge tree. The diagram is the abelianization of the tree, so it forgets the pairing of branches at each confluence: a balanced binary tree and a one-sided caterpillar tree with the same birth values yield identical $H_0$ barcodes but different Strahler distributions. The reading is that $H_0$ at matched density measures the distribution of branching across a network rather than which particular tributary joins which trunk, and this is exactly the invariance that the sensitivity analysis in the Results confirms empirically.

Theorem 3 gives stability with an explicitly limited scope. The diagram is bottleneck-stable as a function of the accumulation field [@cohensteiner2007], so a bounded change in $A$ produces a bounded change in the diagram; but there is no global Lipschitz bound from the input DEM to $A$, because D8 flow direction is discontinuous at saddles, where an arbitrarily small change in elevation can reroute an entire basin. The reading is that the instability from elevation to accumulation is a property of drainage itself, in which small divide migrations reroute basins, and not a defect of the metric. This saddle sensitivity returns as a measured caveat in the Results.

# Two-sample testing framework

## Statistic

We compare a population of $H_0$ diagrams from real terrain with a population from generated terrain using a kernel two-sample test. The distance between two diagrams is the sliced-Wasserstein distance [@carriere2017], which averages one-dimensional Wasserstein distances between projections of the diagrams and is both stable and efficient to compute. From the induced kernel we form the squared maximum mean discrepancy ($\text{MMD}^2$) between the two populations [@gretton2012], a statistic that is zero when the populations are identically distributed and grows as they separate. The kernel bandwidth $\sigma$ is fixed once, by the median heuristic on the real-to-real block of distances ($\sigma = 7.21 \times 10^{6}$ in accumulation-distance units), and held constant across the test and every null replicate, so that all $\text{MMD}^2$ values are computed with the same kernel and are directly comparable. The estimator is implemented as `sliced_wasserstein`, `mmd2_from_matrix`, and `global_sigma` in `src/geo_tda/topo_eval/distributional.py`.

## The spatial by-tile split null (the autocorrelation guard)

The significance of a measured $\text{MMD}^2$ is assessed against a null distribution built by splitting the real data, and for spatially structured data the way the split is formed is decisive. A naive null pools all real patches and divides them at random into two halves. Such a null treats patches as exchangeable, but two patches drawn from the same one-degree tile share geology, climate, and drainage-density regime and belong to the same drainage networks, so they are strongly autocorrelated. A random split scatters same-tile patches across both halves, which makes the halves artificially alike, shrinks the within-null $\text{MMD}^2$, and pushes the floor downward; against that deflated floor almost any statistic clears the bar, and the test becomes anti-conservative.

We instead form the null by splitting spatially. Each null replicate assigns whole tiles to one side and the complementary tiles to the other, so the two halves are as spatially independent of each other as the generated population is of the real one, since the generated patches share no tile with any real patch. The procedure is `spatial_split_null_indexed` in `distributional.py`. The floor it produces is the distribution of $\text{MMD}^2$ between two genuinely independent samples of real terrain at the same spatial granularity, so exceeding it means the generated-to-real divergence is larger than the natural variation between independent real samples, not merely larger than a within-population shuffle.

It is worth stating plainly what the spatial split costs and what it buys, because the correct description is a correctly calibrated floor rather than a tighter one. The independent units in this design are tiles, not patches, so the effective sample is about ten independent units per side, and the roughly $113$ patches per side reported below are drawn from those ten tiles. Holding out whole tiles raises the central location of the floor, because it now reflects genuine between-sample independence, and at the same time it widens the floor's sampling variability, because fewer truly independent units stand behind the estimate. These two effects act in opposite directions, so the honest claim is one of validity rather than precision: the by-tile split trades some estimation precision for a floor that is correctly calibrated to real spatial independence, and the headline divergence clears that wider, properly calibrated band. The floor is estimated from $200$ random balanced by-tile splits (`m2_generated_vs_real.json`), a sampled subset of the $\binom{20}{10}/2 = 92{,}378$ possible balanced splits rather than the full enumeration.

## Operating point and calibration

The operating point is the largest balanced spatial split the design allows, ten tiles per side out of twenty, which yields about $113$ patches per side. Choosing the largest balanced split uses the most tile-independent data available while preserving the spatial independence of the two halves, so power and independence are balanced rather than traded against each other (@fig-power). Two distinct floors appear in what follows, and we keep them separate. A pre-registered design floor, the null 95th percentile of $0.0263$ estimated before any terrain was generated (`m2_power.json`), was used to fix the generation target and to confirm that the design was adequately powered. A realized floor, the null 95th percentile of $0.0283$ computed alongside the headline test on the same real data (`m2_generated_vs_real.json`), is the denominator of the headline ratio reported below. The two values are close, they serve different roles, and we do not substitute one for the other.

![Spatial-null MMD$^2$ p95 floor versus sample size; the by-tile null tightens monotonically and the K=10 operating point (about 113 patches per side) is fixed before generation.](figures/fig-power.png){#fig-power}

# Data and study area

The study region lies in the eastern United States and spans three physiographic provinces with contrasting drainage character: the Cumberland Plateau, a dissected sandstone plateau cut by deep, steep-walled valleys; the Appalachian Highlands, with their parallel ridges and intervening valleys; and the Coastal Plain, a low-relief landscape of forests and wetlands. The first two provinces are karst-bearing, with solution features developed in the underlying carbonates; the Coastal Plain is not karst, and is included precisely to span a contrasting, low-relief drainage regime. We scope every validity claim to this setting, three physiographic provinces of the conterminous United States, and do not generalize past it; glaciated, arid-endorheic, and other regimes are untested.

Two elevation products are used. Construct validity is established on the USGS 3D Elevation Program (3DEP) seamless DEM [@usgs3dep], and the generated-to-real comparison uses the Copernicus GLO-30 global DEM [@copernicusdem], which is the elevation domain on which the MESA generator was trained. The metric is resolution-invariant at matched drainage density, which we confirm by comparing 3DEP and GLO-30 over the same footprints: across the probe tiles the median elevation correlation is $0.987$ and the median net Strahler-Wasserstein distance between the two extracted networks is $0.017$ (`resolution_probe.json`), so the GLO-30 comparison is not confounded by resolution. The reference drainage network throughout is the USGS National Hydrography Dataset (NHD) flowline product [@usgsnhd], which also fixes the common target drainage density of $1.732$ km km$^{-2}$ used to calibrate $\tau$. NHD flowlines map surface drainage and do not trace subsurface conduit flow, so the reference shares the surface-only blindness of our DEM-derived network: in the karst-bearing provinces both miss any flow that goes underground, which keeps the comparison symmetric and fair while scoping the claim to surface drainage.

The corpus is twenty one-degree tiles selected to span the NHD drainage-density quintiles of the region, in a province mix of seven Cumberland, seven Appalachian, and six Coastal tiles (`tile_manifest.json`). Each tile is cut into non-overlapping $768$-pixel patches, about $23$ km on a side; of these, $226$ patches yield a valid non-degenerate $H_0$ diagram (a per-tile yield of $7$ to $14$, mean $11.3$), with flat or open-water windows excluded because they carry no drainage topology to compare.

Generated terrain comes from MESA [@bornepons2025mesa], prompted per province with descriptions of the corresponding landscape. To prevent province composition from confounding the two-sample comparison, the generated batch is a forced mirror of the real reference mix: $40$ Cumberland, $40$ Appalachian, and $34$ Coastal patches, for $114$ generated patches at $768$ pixels (`mesa_batch113_manifest.json`).^[The manifest filename retains the earlier label `113` from a provisional batch size; the file itself holds $114$ patches, which is the count used throughout. A clean rename of the artifact is deferred to the archive-DOI pass to avoid churning result paths now.] All processing was scoped to hardware available to the authors, a single $12$ GB GPU, with full-degree tiles handled by windowing; we report this hardware floor explicitly so that the sample sizes and resolutions are understood as deliberate, reproducible choices rather than incidental ones. @fig-studyarea shows the tile selection by province and drainage-density quintile.

![The 20 one-degree study tiles colored by physiographic province; labels give the NHD drainage-density quintile.](figures/fig-studyarea.png){#fig-studyarea}

# Results

## Construct validity against NHD (three CONUS provinces of contrasting drainage character)

Before applying the metric to generated terrain we establish that it measures real drainage organization, on the twenty-tile real corpus and scoped to the three sampled physiographic provinces of the conterminous United States. The test was pre-registered against an agreement ceiling: how well the field-standard flow-accumulation extractor (WhiteboxTools) reproduces the independently mapped NHD network on three branching criteria. Our persistence-based extraction matches NHD essentially as well as the field-standard tool does. It reaches a junction-count correlation of $0.936$ against the ceiling of $0.935$; a Strahler Wasserstein-1 distance of $0.287$, equal to the ceiling; and an absolute bifurcation-ratio difference of $0.185$, better than the ceiling of $0.375$ (lower is better on the latter two). All three pre-registered criteria pass, and the persistence extraction meets or beats the field-standard tool on each (@tbl-construct; `construct.json`). We restrict this validity claim to the three sampled provinces, two karst-bearing and one (the Coastal Plain) not; it does not extend to glaciated or arid-endorheic regimes, which were not tested.

The donor-graph adjacency contributes here in a concrete way. A merge tree built on grid (8-connected) adjacency admits a median of $48{,}498$ spurious one-dimensional cycles per tile, where channels run close together without being hydrologically connected; the donor graph removes all of them by construction, leaving a forest with first Betti number zero.

{{< include _tables/tbl-construct.md >}}

## Substrate comparability and statistical power

Two checks precede the headline comparison. The first confirms that generated terrain is a valid input to the drainage comparison rather than a degenerate surface that would separate trivially. MESA outputs normalized elevation on the unit interval, but because the metric is scale-invariant this is immaterial, and on eleven scale-free routing descriptors the median MESA value falls inside the real $[p_5, p_{95}]$ band for every one (`substrate_comparability.json`). A divergence in the diagrams therefore reflects a difference in drainage organization and not a substrate artifact. The second check is statistical power: the spatial-null floor tightens monotonically as the per-side sample grows, reaching the design floor of $0.0263$ at the ten-tile operating point (@fig-power), so the design was confirmed to be adequately powered before any terrain was generated.

## Headline: generated drainage topology is distinguishable from real

At matched drainage density, the population of $H_0$ diagrams from MESA-generated terrain is distinguishable from the real population by the sliced-Wasserstein-kernel $\text{MMD}^2$ tested against the spatial by-tile null. The test median $\text{MMD}^2$ is $0.0885$ against a realized floor of $0.0283$, a ratio of $3.12$, and none of the $200$ real-to-real null splits reaches the test value (one-sided $p < 0.005$), on $226$ real and $114$ generated patches (@tbl-headline, @fig-headline; `m2_generated_vs_real.json`). @fig-qualitative shows the effect concretely: a real patch and a MESA patch of comparable hillshade appearance carry visibly different channel networks and persistence diagrams, the looks-fine-but-routes-differently pattern the metric is designed to catch.

This result is distributional distinguishability of drainage topology rather than proven gross network failure, and we are careful to keep two different quantities, reported in two different currencies, from being conflated. The headline ratio of $3.12$ is a distributional separation: it is the test median $\text{MMD}^2$ divided by the spatial-null p95 floor, and it measures how far the generated population sits from real terrain relative to the natural variation between independent real samples. A second, smaller ratio measures something else, the raw magnitude of diagram movement: the mean generated-to-real sliced-Wasserstein $H_0$ distance is about $1.27$ times the mean real-to-real distance (`m2_raw_movement.json`). The two are not the same number in the same units, and neither is a "saddle fraction": neither attributes any share of the divergence to saddle rerouting. What the raw movement does establish is one of magnitude. A movement of about $1.27$ times the real-to-real baseline sits within the regime where D8 saddle rerouting alone already moves the diagram by a comparable amount. The pre-MESA saddle probe makes this concrete (`saddle_probe_A.json`): the 95th percentile of saddle-induced $H_0$ movement, expressed in the same real-to-real-baseline units, reaches about $2.79$, and in the high-flip regime that MESA's pervasive surface change occupies the median per-tile movement runs from about $1.21$ to $1.35$ times the baseline. Saddle sensitivity, established in Theorem 3, is therefore part of what the headline movement measures, and the $1.27$ raw movement cannot on its own be read as gross macro-scale network failure. Separating macro-scale drainage divergence from saddle-driven movement is left to a saddle-stable variant of the statistic in future work.

{{< include _tables/tbl-headline.md >}}

![Null (real vs real) versus test (generated vs real) MMD$^2$ at the K=10 operating point; the test median sits at 3.12x the p95 floor and no null split exceeds it.](figures/fig-headline.png){#fig-headline}

![Looks fine, routes wrong: a real GLO-30 patch and a MESA-generated patch of comparable appearance, with visibly different channel networks and H0 persistence diagrams.](figures/fig-qualitative.png){#fig-qualitative}

## Optical contrast: every metric separates, only topology names the axis

To place the topological result against the metrics the field actually uses, we ran the same two-sample comparison with standard optical metrics on rendered hillshade and geomorphic-stack images, at the same operating point and on the identical $114$ generated patches, each optical metric judged against its own real-to-real spatial null. The real sample sizes differ between the two families, $226$ patches for topology and $320$ for the optical metrics, because the topological metric is defined only on windows that yield a valid non-degenerate $H_0$ diagram (flat and open-water windows are excluded) whereas every window renders to a valid image. This is the natural domain of each metric rather than a mismatch, since each test is judged against its own matched null. The optical positive control behaves as it should: the optical metrics separate the Coastal province from the two mountainous provinces but do not separate the two mountainous provinces from one another, confirming that the instrument tracks genuine geomorphic appearance.

Every optical metric separates generated from real, and the stronger backbone separates it cleanly. The RBF-MMD test-to-floor ratio is $4.36$ for CLIP on hillshade and $4.30$ for CLIP on the geomorphic stack, against $1.70$ and $2.68$ for Inception; the kernel Inception distance gives $6.72$, $5.03$, $1.40$, and $2.10$ on the same four settings (@tbl-optical, @fig-optical; `m2_optical_contrast.json`). The 2048-dimensional Fréchet Inception Distance is unstable at this sample size and is reported through the kernel distance instead. The honest reading is not that the optical metrics are blind: they detect a real and substantial appearance gap, and CLIP in fact separates generated from real more strongly than it separates real provinces from one another. But that gap is unlocalized. It is entangled with overall geomorphic appearance and does not identify which property changed, and the field-standard Inception backbone is the weakest detector here, consistent with the finding of @kynkaanniemi2023 that Inception features track ImageNet categories rather than domain structure. The flow-accumulation metric adds what these scores cannot: it names the axis of difference as topographic surface drainage organization, validated against NHD.

{{< include _tables/tbl-optical.md >}}

![Optical test/floor ratios (RBF-MMD and KID) for CLIP and Inception, with the province-swap positive control and the topology reference at 3.12x; every optical metric separates generated from real.](figures/fig-optical.png){#fig-optical}

## Sensitivity profile of the metric

To characterize what the metric reads, we measured its response to three controlled perturbations of real terrain, all expressed in the same test-to-floor currency as the headline (@tbl-sensitivity, @fig-sensitivity). A localized incision, a randomized trench carved into a patch, leaves the $H_0$ response essentially at the floor across the full sweep of trench sizes, even as the optical metrics register the gouge (CLIP on the stack rising to about $1.65$ and Inception to about $2.99$); the topological metric is invariant to a single localized megachannel (`m2_power_curve.json`). A phase-randomized Fourier surrogate, which destroys all spatial coherence while preserving the power spectrum exactly, gives a response of $2.82$, below the MESA value (`m2_noise_vs_real.json`). MESA itself gives $3.12$, the largest of the three.

These three measurements form a sensitivity profile, not a bracket, and in particular MESA is the largest response rather than an intermediate one, so we establish no upper bound on the metric. The two perturbations bound the metric's invariance from opposite directions and converge on a single reading: at matched density the metric responds to pervasive reorganization of the branching distribution, and not to localized features or to spatial coherence as such. The phase-randomized surrogate is informative precisely because it reads as closer to real than MESA does: scrambling phase preserves the branching distribution that the metric is built to read, whereas MESA, by over-smoothing the surface, changes that distribution itself. The same logic explains the invariance to the trench, which alters one channel without changing how branching is distributed across the network.

{{< include _tables/tbl-sensitivity.md >}}

![Sensitivity profile in matched MMD$^2$/floor currency (trench, phase-noise, MESA) and the saddle-flip caveat; MESA is the largest response and no upper bound is established.](figures/fig-sensitivity.png){#fig-sensitivity}

# Discussion

The contribution of this work is interpretive rather than a claim of greater detection power. Across the metrics we evaluated, both the optical scores and the flow-accumulation metric distinguish MESA-generated terrain from real terrain, so the value of the topological regime is not that it sees a difference the others miss. Its value is that it names the difference. An optical metric returns a single number that says generated and real terrain differ in fidelity; the flow-accumulation metric returns a number on a specific, validated axis and says the difference is one of topographic surface drainage organization. For any downstream use that cares about how a synthesized surface routes water, a physically named discrepancy is more actionable than an unlocalized embedding distance, and it is this localization, grounded in the bijection with the channel network and the agreement with NHD, that the regime contributes.

This reframing also fixes the role of the optical metrics in the comparison. We treat them as a contextualized baseline rather than a foil to be beaten. They are strong detectors of appearance, in places stronger than the topological metric, and we make no attempt to win a sensitivity contest against vision feature spaces. The complementary claim is the defensible one: appearance metrics detect that something changed, and the drainage metric identifies what.

Several limitations bound the claim and are worth stating plainly. First, the measured divergence carries a component of saddle sensitivity. As Theorem 3 makes precise, the diagram is stable in the accumulation field but not Lipschitz in the input elevation, because D8 routing is discontinuous at saddles, and the raw movement of $H_0$ under the generated comparison sits at about $1.27$ times the real-to-real baseline, a magnitude that saddle rerouting alone can reach (the pre-MESA probe puts saddle-induced movement at a 95th percentile of about $2.79$ baselines and a high-flip median of about $1.21$ to $1.35$ baselines). This raw-movement ratio is a magnitude statement, not an attribution: it is not a fraction of the divergence assigned to saddles, and it is reported in a different currency from the $3.12$ distributional-separation ratio of the headline. Separating macro-scale drainage divergence from saddle-driven movement is the natural next refinement, through a saddle-stable variant of the statistic, and we leave it to future work. Second, the sensitivity analysis is a profile and not a bound: it characterizes the metric's response to three perturbation classes and shows that the metric reads pervasive branching-distribution structure, but it does not establish an upper bound on the response, and the generated case is in fact the largest response we measured. Third, the empirical demonstration uses a single generator as a worked case study; a multi-generator benchmark is separate work and is deliberately out of scope here. Fourth, the validation is scoped to three physiographic provinces of the conterminous United States, two karst-bearing and one (the Coastal Plain) not, and we do not claim it for glaciated, arid-endorheic, or other regimes that were not tested. Fifth, the effective sample size of the null is set by independent tiles, about ten per side, rather than by the larger patch count, which widens the floor's sampling variability even as it makes the floor correctly calibrated.

A note on sequencing closes the discussion. The evaluation regime is presented and published on its own, ahead of any generative model that might later be trained or selected with a related quantity, so that the metric cannot be read as bespoke to a particular generator. Keeping the metric independent of the models it will judge is what licenses its use as an evaluator in that downstream setting.

# Conclusions

We have introduced a drainage-aware evaluation regime for generative terrain: the $H_0$ persistence of a donor-graph merge tree built on D8 flow accumulation, compared between populations of real and generated patches at a calibrated drainage density with a sliced-Wasserstein-kernel two-sample test against a spatial by-tile null. A small theorem stack ties the construction to standard drainage analysis, and the construct is validated against independently mapped NHD flowlines across three physiographic provinces of contrasting drainage character. Applied to MESA as a worked case study, the regime confirms that generated and real terrain are distinguishable and, unlike the appearance metrics it sits beside, names the axis of the difference as topographic surface drainage organization. We characterize the metric's sensitivity honestly, bounding what it reads through controlled perturbations, and we are explicit about saddle sensitivity, geographic scope, and the single-generator setting. The result is an evaluator that reports a geomorphically meaningful quantity rather than an unlocalized score, which is what an assessment of synthetic terrain should provide for any use that depends on how the surface drains.

# Appendix A: proofs

<!-- Port from docs/topo_eval/proofs/strahler_merge_tree.qmd. SUBMISSION BLOCKER:
the proof referee-pass is a hard pre-submission gate (the theorem stack is
headline novelty), named alongside the Cousty-Najman 2019 (JMIV) hardcopy
novelty skim. Not a finishing step. -->

- Lemma (bijection), Theorem 1 (Strahler coarsening), Theorem 2 (PD abelianization), Theorem 3 (field-level stability with honest scope).

# Code and data availability

- Code: `src/geo_tda/topo_eval/` and `scripts/`. Result JSONs: `results/validity/`. [Add repository DOI / archive on submission.]
- Data: 3DEP and GLO-30 DEMs and NHD flowlines are public (USGS, Copernicus); the MESA batch is reproducible from `scripts/mesa_generate.py`.

# Author contributions

- [TBD]

# Competing interests

- [TBD]

# Acknowledgements

- [TBD. GACRC compute; advisor input.]
